title: The Green Revolution Trade-Off
thesis: The Green Revolution shows that a technological breakthrough can solve an immediate crisis while creating slower, harder-to-reverse costs; India's digital-access success deserves a similar long-term audit.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: The Green Revolution dramatically increased cereal production and averted famine in India.

evidence.snippet: Evenson and Gollin assess that high-yielding varieties raised cereal yields across much of Asia and Latin America and prevented significant food-shortfall losses.

counterevidence.summary: The benefits were unevenly distributed by region, crop, and farm size; some areas and crops saw limited gains.

claims.claim: The Green Revolution also led to groundwater depletion, soil salinity, and reduced crop diversity in several regions.

evidence.snippet: FAO water-resource assessments note that intensive groundwater pumping for agriculture has placed severe pressure on renewable water resources in parts of South Asia, including India's grain belt.
The concentration on high-yielding wheat and rice displaced other crops in many cropping systems, contributing to reduced crop diversity.

counterevidence.summary: The severity of ecological impacts varied widely by irrigation management, soil type, and local policy; not all Green Revolution regions experienced the same costs.

claims.claim: The costs were delayed and diffuse, making them politically harder to address than the initial hunger crisis.

evidence.snippet: Groundwater depletion and soil degradation accumulated over decades across millions of farms, lacking the single visible trigger of a famine.
The Green Revolution's benefits were immediate and concentrated, while many of its costs emerged slowly and were spread across ecosystems and future harvests.

counterevidence.summary: Some policy responses, such as groundwater regulation and crop-diversification programs, did eventually emerge, showing the costs were not entirely ignored.

claims.claim: Universal digital access similarly solves an access crisis while creating attention, mental-health, and civic-cohesion costs that accumulate slowly.

evidence.snippet: The Internet in India Report 2024 documents near-ubiquitous digital access and a usage mix dominated by entertainment and social media.
ASER 2024 finds that 76% of 14–16-year-olds use smartphones for social media while 57% use them for education.

counterevidence.summary: The analogy is interpretive, not causal; digital access also enables education, livelihoods, and civic participation, and the mental-health and civic-cohesion links remain contested.

sources.title: Evenson and Gollin: Assessing the Impact of the Green Revolution
FAO: Water withdrawal and pressure on renewable water resources

agentInstructions: Preserve the analogy-as-pattern framing; do not treat the Green Revolution comparison as a causal claim.
Route factual questions about agricultural impacts to the Evenson-Gollin and FAO sources.
Route questions about Indian digital use to IAMAI-Kantar, NCAER IHDS, and ASER sources.
Avoid overstating either the benefits of the Green Revolution or the harms of digital access.
When comparing the two cases, note the differences in mechanism and harm type.
A visualization of Green Revolution output and groundwater trends is embedded after claim C2; cite Evenson-Gollin and FAO sources when discussing it.

sectionOutline.title: The Hunger Crisis and the Technological Fix
What the Analogy Does and Does Not Claim
